svg.text: CVD Visualization Repair Tool: Decision Tree
Separate data marks (bars, areas, bubbles), legend swatches,
and non-data elements (axes, labels, gridlines, titles)
(heuristic cue about designer intent, not ground truth)
(what the data actually looks like, independent of palette)
Count categories. Examine legend/axis labels (strings vs. numbers).
Analyze color usage distribution (clustered? skewed? full range?)
If diverging: validate midpoint semantics (is it 0, mean, median?)
Phase 4: Reconcile Palette Type vs. Data Signals
A: Repair assuming designer's palette type
Phase 5: Simulate Under CVD, Test Invariants, Repair
Simulate palette under target CVD type (Machado et al. 2009)
Pick max min pairwise Delta E
Edge: labels are numeric, possible mismatch, route to Phase 4
Only runs when hue diversity > 40 (multi-hue palettes)
Apply repaired palette to SVG DOM
Open Questions and Edge Cases and other random thoughts
Multiple simultaneous failures. A palette can fail several invariants at once. Fix in order: midpoint, then monotonicity, then uniformity, then step size.
Data distribution. Cannot move midpoint relative to other points because data may be unevenly distributed. Repairs that look good on the palette
in isolation might distort the data mapping.
Dynamic vs. fixed repair menu. Current ideas proposes from fixed set of replacement palettes. More sophisticated version would generate palettes?
LLM integration points. Palette classification (Phase 2), midpoint validation (Div. pre-check), mismatch detection (Phase 4) are candidates for LLM reasoning.
